name: Kaumodaki
description: Kaumodaki is the divine mace of Vishnu, one of the principal deities of Hinduism. It is depicted as a powerful and imposing weapon, symbolizing Vishnu's strength and ability to uphold Dharma (righteousness) and destroy evil. The mace is not merely a weapon of brute force but also represents the power of knowledge and the ability to dispel ignorance. Its origin is often attributed to the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana), where it emerged along with other divine treasures. Alternatively, some texts suggest it was crafted by Vishwakarma, the divine architect. Kaumodaki's significance lies in its association with Vishnu's role as the preserver and protector of the universe. It is wielded by Vishnu in his various avatars, including Rama and Krishna, to vanquish demonic forces and restore cosmic balance. The mace's destructive power is tempered by Vishnu's inherent compassion and justice, ensuring that it is used only for the greater good. The Kaumodaki is a tangible representation of Vishnu's unwavering commitment to safeguarding the universe and its inhabitants from the forces of chaos and darkness, serving as a constant reminder of the triumph of good over evil and the enduring power of righteousness. The mace is often described as being made of iron or other indestructible materials, and its impact is said to be capable of shattering mountains and obliterating armies. Its presence instills fear in the hearts of the wicked and inspires hope in the righteous.
subtitle: Vishnu's Divine Mace of Power
origin: Churning of the Ocean or crafted by Vishwakarma
currentLocation: With Vishnu in Vaikuntha or his various avatars

keyMyths.title: Churning of the Ocean
keyMyths.description: Kaumodaki emerged during the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the ocean, signifying its divine origin and association with Vishnu.

keyMyths.title: Krishna and Kaumodaki
keyMyths.description: Krishna, an avatar of Vishnu, wields the Kaumodaki to defeat various demons and uphold Dharma in the Mahabharata.

sources.author: Swami Chinmayananda
sources.description: Commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, discussing Vishnu's role and attributes.
sources.text: Commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, discussing Vishnu's role and attributes.
